msgs.text:
Turn 29 — the “full ghost-kernel surjectivity” target is too strong I used only the three files attached to this turn. The original target remains the over-G isomorphism question for ΓS,ΓH, namely whether there is a continuous Φ:ΓS→ΓH with πH∘Φ=πS.The current gap is exactly the one left in Turn 28: the residual Ef,mod2,s=1 obstruction tower is already closed, but finite coefficient lifting over

R:=EfΛ≃Z2 [[t]],t=s−1,still needs a coefficient-level argument.

The result of this turn is:The Turn 28 surjectivity target is too strong as stated.

More precisely, if one interpretsΘd,r:Kd,r↠Qd,r as saying that the zero-lower-ghost kernel alone should hit the whole restricted-square quotient in every root degree, then the statement is already false in the first associated-graded model. This is not a disproof of ΓS≅GΓH. It says the coefficient induction must be phrased as a ghost-pair exactness statement, not as pure kernel-square surjectivity.

Recall the residual correction boundary:∂d:Ld⊕Ld→Ld+1,(U,V)↦[A,U]+[B,V],where L∙⊂F2⟨⟨A,B⟩⟩ is the free restricted Lie algebra. The residual induction usesLn/im∂n−1≅{0,Ln/2[2],n odd,n even,and Turn 18 proved Cm=Lm for all m≥5, so the residual tower itself is closed.Now impose the first 2-ghost-zero condition from the coefficient problem. OverA2=R/(4,t),the dangerous Ef-row becomes (s2+1,s−1)=(2,0), so the lower 2-ghost sees the Y0 -coordinate. Setting that ghost to zero removes the Y0 -coordinate at first order. The raw ghost-zero boundary is therefore∂d(0):Ld⊕Ld→Ld+1,(U,V)↦[A,U]+[B,V],where U,V are placed in the Y1 - and q-coordinates. Its kernel is the optimistic first approximation to Kd,r.

The associated square-shadow map isθd:ker∂d(0)⟶L2d/im∂2d−1≅Ld[2],(U,V)⟼U[2]+V[2].

The rank test gives:d45678dimLd66111836 dimker∂d(0)634616dimLd[2]66111836 rankθd534615 So even the optimistic ghost-zero kernel does not hit the full restricted-square quotient. The failure is not a one-off. It grows immediately after d=4.

This explains why Turn 27’s seed q↦qY4 was enough for the first bottleneck but cannot be the whole coefficient induction. Turn 27 only needed to hit the one missing line left by the high torsor at the degree-16 obstruction; the qY4 seed did that because its square shadow is B8 and it has zero 2-ghost.But demanding that the same kind of zero-ghost kernel hit all of Ld[2]is a stronger statement, and the rank table shows it is false.The structural correction is the following.

The coefficient lift should not be governed byθd (kergd)=?Qd.

It should be governed by the full Witt ghost-pair mapWd:Td⟶Ld⊕Q2d,where Td is the full degree-d lower-plus-high correction space andWd (z)=(gd (z),θd (z)).For the 2-direction, a correction with primitive initial form W contributes the coupled pair (2W,W[2]),not merely a square shadow. Turn 25 already warned that group-likeness alone does not force this compatibility; the actual Nielsen torsor must be tracked.

Turn 29 sharpens that warning: even after imposing zero lower ghost, the kernel-square map is too small, so the coefficient induction cannot be a pure kernel-surjectivity theorem.

The right condition is instead:Every actual coefficient defect (U,Ω) must lie in the image of the ghost-pair map Wdmodulo the already high-controllable directions.Equivalently, after choosing a correction that kills the lower ghost U, the remaining square defect isΩ+U[2],and only this defect class needs to lie in the square-shadow image of the ghost-zero kernel. This is exactly the formulation Turn 25 suggested:δ2 (U,Ω)=Ω+U[2].The point is that U is not usually zero. Forcing U=0 at the outset loses the coupled Witt information.This also explains why the t-direction remains structurally different. OverAt=R/(2,t2),the row is (0,t), so the first t-ghost is derivational rather than Frobenius-square. The t-case should be included in the same ghost-pair complex, but it should not be expected to behave like the 2-Bockstein square map.So Turn 29 does not find a new obstruction. It removes a false proof target.

The current state is now cleaner:residual tower closed+coefficient compactness framework available+coefficient lift reduced to ghost-pair exactness.

The corrected coefficient induction should use the complexTdWdLd⊕Q2d,together with the high affine torsor, rather than the smaller mapkergd→Q2d.In particular, the next computation should not ask whether the zero-ghost kernel hits all of Ld[2]. It should compute the cokernel of the pair mapWd=(gd,θd)after quotienting by the already high-controllable residual image. The first meaningful test is whether the actual A2 -defect classes vanish in that cokernel for d=4,5,6,7,8. If they do, the observed pattern can be promoted to an m=(2,t)-adic coefficient induction. If one survives, that would be the first serious finite-coefficient obstruction candidate.

ESTABLISHED: The raw Turn-28 surjectivity statement is false if interpreted as “the zero-lower-ghost kernel alone maps onto the full restricted-square quotient.” In root degrees d=4,5,6,7,8, the ranks of the raw square-shadow map are 5,3,4,6,15, while the target dimensions are 6,6,11,18,36. Thus coefficient lifting must use the full ghost-pair map, not pure kernel-square surjectivity.

GAP: The finite coefficient nonemptiness lemma remains unproved. The precise remaining question is whether the actual coefficient defects (U,Ω) lie in the image of the full ghost-pair map Wd=(gd,θd), modulo the already high-controllable residual directions, for every coefficient grade and root degree.

NEXT: Compute the first ghost-pair cokernel, beginning over A2=R/(4,t), for d=4,5,6,7,8. Do not impose U=0 prematurely. Track the paired defect Ω+U[2]; if the actual defects vanish in the pair-cokernel, formulate the m-adic induction using ghost-pair exactness rather than kernel-square surjectivity.[TURN29-GHOST-KERNEL-SURJECTIVITY-TOO-STRONG]
